questions.question: Explain the difference between preemptive and non-preemptive scheduling. Give one example of each.
keyPoints: Preemptive allows forced removal of CPU from running process
Non-preemptive lets process run until it voluntarily yields CPU
Examples: Round Robin (preemptive), FCFS (non-preemptive)
Preemptive provides better response time for interactive systems

questions.question: Define and explain any three scheduling criteria used to evaluate CPU scheduling algorithms.
keyPoints: CPU Utilization: percentage of time CPU is busy
Throughput: processes completed per unit time
Turnaround Time: total time from arrival to completion
Waiting Time: total time spent in ready queue
Response Time: time from arrival to first CPU allocation

questions.question: Explain the three types of schedulers in an operating system with their functions.
keyPoints: Long-term scheduler: controls degree of multiprogramming, admits processes to ready queue
Short-term scheduler: selects process from ready queue for CPU execution
Medium-term scheduler: handles swapping, temporarily removes processes from memory

questions.question: What is a context switch? Explain its role in process scheduling.
keyPoints: Context switch is saving/restoring CPU state between processes
Involves saving registers, program counter, stack pointer
Takes place when switching from one process to another
Adds overhead to system performance

questions.question: Differentiate between CPU-bound and I/O-bound processes. Why is it important for a scheduler to consider this difference?
keyPoints: CPU-bound: long CPU bursts, minimal I/O
I/O-bound: short CPU bursts, frequent I/O waits
Scheduler balances both types for optimal CPU utilization
I/O-bound processes need quick CPU access to initiate I/O operations

questions.question: What is dispatch latency? How does it affect system performance?
keyPoints: Dispatch latency is time taken by dispatcher to stop one process and start another
Includes context switching time
Reduces effective CPU time available for processes
Lower dispatch latency improves system responsiveness

questions.question: Define turnaround time, waiting time, and response time with formulas.
keyPoints: Turnaround Time = Completion Time - Arrival Time
Waiting Time = Turnaround Time - Burst Time
Response Time = First CPU Assignment Time - Arrival Time
These metrics measure different aspects of process scheduling performance

questions.question:
Consider the following processes with their arrival times and burst times:
Process | Arrival Time | Burst Time
P1 | 0 | 8
P2 | 1 | 4
P3 | 2 | 2
P4 | 3 | 6

Calculate the average waiting time and average turnaround time using FCFS scheduling algorithm.
keyPoints: FCFS order: P1, P2, P3, P4 based on arrival times
Calculate completion times: P1=8, P2=12, P3=14, P4=20
Turnaround times: P1=8, P2=11, P3=12, P4=17
Waiting times: P1=0, P2=7, P3=10, P4=11
Average Waiting Time = (0+7+10+11)/4 = 7
Average Turnaround Time = (8+11+12+17)/4 = 12

questions.question: Explain the Round Robin scheduling algorithm. What is a time quantum? Discuss the effect of time quantum size on system performance.
keyPoints: Round Robin allocates CPU to processes in circular order
Each process gets time quantum for execution
If process doesn't complete, it's moved to end of ready queue
Small time quantum: better response time but more context switches
Large time quantum: fewer switches but poor response time
Optimal quantum balances both - typically 10-100ms

questions.question: Explain the Shortest Job First (SJF) scheduling algorithm. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages. Why is it difficult to implement in practice?
keyPoints: SJF selects process with shortest next CPU burst
Provides minimum average waiting time among all algorithms
Two types: preemptive (SRTF) and non-preemptive
Disadvantage: longer processes may suffer starvation
Difficulty: hard to predict actual burst time in practice
Burst time estimation needed using historical data

questions.question: What are the issues in multiprocessor scheduling? Explain any two approaches to multiprocessor scheduling.
keyPoints: Issues: load balancing, processor affinity, cache coherence
Approaches: load sharing and load balancing
Load sharing: one queue shared by all processors
Load balancing: distributes work evenly across processors
Processor affinity: process prefers same processor for cache benefits
Hard scheduling complexity with multiple CPUs
